nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef slopes, lagoons, bays, and boat channels; typically found on rock and rubble in lower to middle reef zones with reduced light and moderate nutrient levels

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Absorbs dissolved organic nutrients from the water column. Benefits from supplemental spot feeding of small meaty foods — brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, or micro-plankton — approximately once or twice per week. Feeding is optional but tends to improve growth and coloration, particularly in low-nutrient systems.

goodWith: clownfish (may use as host anemone substitute)
other Rhodactis mushrooms with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals in direct contact (torch, hammer, frogspawn, chalice)
large sweeper-tentacle SPS corals without spacing
large urchins that may dislodge frags
digging or sand-sifting fish near placement

summary: Rhodactis Mushroom Corals are a genus of skeleton-free corals in the order Corallimorpharia, generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly coral groups. They tend to thrive under low to moderate light and gentle flow on lower rockwork, and are available in a wide range of vivid trade colorations.
careNotes:
Rhodactis is a genus of corallimorph corals in the order Corallimorpharia, family Discosomatidae. Unlike true stony corals, they lack a calcium carbonate skeleton. The genus is commonly sold in the trade under names such as 'Bullseye Mushroom,' 'Superman Mushroom,' and various color-trade names. This entry covers the non-hairy Rhodactis species most commonly sold in the aquarium trade, principally R. inchoata and R. mussoides; the companion entry for Hairy Mushroom Coral covers R. indosinensis.

The two species most frequently encountered in the hobby differ noticeably in size and appearance. R. inchoata (Carlgren, 1943) — the coral most often sold as 'Bullseye Mushroom' or 'Blue Mushroom' — produces smaller polyps typically ranging from 5 to 15 cm in diameter and is known for its bold circular disc patterning. R. mussoides (Duchassaing & Michelotti, 1864) grows considerably larger, with somewhat folded polyps reaching 20 to 25 cm in diameter, an olive-green body, and a white or pink oral area; it can occasionally show a lightly textured surface, though it is distinct from the densely tentacled R. indosinensis. A third species, R. musciformis (listed in some literature as R. osculifera), has a Caribbean distribution and is less commonly encountered in the trade. Many specimens are sold simply as 'Rhodactis sp.' and cannot be reliably identified to species level from photographs alone, so genus-level guidance covers most husbandry situations.

Lighting: Low to moderate PAR is generally advised, with an approximate range of 50–150 PAR and a commonly cited sweet spot around 75–100 PAR. New specimens should be acclimated gradually, starting at the lower end and adjusting upward if coloration or disc expansion is poor.

Flow: Low flow is preferred. Direct powerhead current has been documented causing tissue stress, shrinking, and detachment. Indirect or gently alternating flow is recommended.

Placement: Lower to middle tank zones are generally advised, where light and flow are gentler.

Aggression: Rhodactis is considered semi-aggressive and has been known to sting and overgrow neighboring corals on direct contact. Clear spacing from LPS (large-polyp stony) and other corals is recommended. In high-density mushroom tanks, allelopathic chemical release — where the coral releases compounds that can stress nearby animals — may affect neighbors; running activated carbon is sometimes recommended as a countermeasure.

Predation: Rhodactis species can engulf small invertebrates and food items by expanding and closing their disc. This behavior is generally not a threat to passing fish under normal aquarium conditions.

Calcium and alkalinity requirements are lower than those of stony corals since no skeletal deposition occurs; stable parameters tend to matter more than achieving precision highs.

Handling: Standard reef precautions apply — gloves are recommended, and hands should be washed after any contact. Rhodactis possess nematocysts (stinging cells) that can cause skin irritation.

Reproduction: Reproduces asexually via longitudinal fission and pedal laceration (in which small fragments shed from the base of the polyp develop into new individuals). Fragging by cutting is practiced in the hobby; cut pieces placed on rubble or frag plugs in a low-flow area will typically attach within days.

Host behavior: Clownfish have been documented using large Rhodactis colonies as anemone substitutes, though this behavior is less commonly observed than with R. indosinensis.

Adult size spread: R. inchoata typically reaches 10–20 cm (approximately 4–8 inches) in diameter; R. mussoides can reach 20–25 cm (approximately 8–10 inches) or slightly larger. Across these species, adult polyps range from approximately 5 cm in smaller forms to 25 cm in the largest.
breedingNotes: Reproduces asexually in captivity via longitudinal fission (developing two mouths, then dividing) and pedal laceration (fragments of the basal disc developing into new polyps). Fragging by cutting mature specimens is a commonly practiced propagation method. Cut pieces are typically placed on rubble or frag plugs in a low-flow area with indirect light to recover. Sexual reproduction occurs on natural reefs but is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Purple Bullseye Rhodactis Mushroom Coral
primary.notes: Primary species page for Rhodactis inchoata (Purple Bullseye). Provided temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.023–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (Easy), placement (Bottom), flow (Low), temperament (Semi-Aggressive), diet (zooxanthellae + micro-plankton/brine shrimp), adult size (4 inches diameter).

additional.name: TFH Magazine – Rhodactis mussoides
additional.notes: Cross-checked R. mussoides specifically. Temperature 72–83°F (22–28°C), pH 8.1–8.2, salinity 1.025, calcium 380–430 ppm, alkalinity 2.5 meq/L (~7 dKH), low-moderate light with some current required. Adult size 8–10 inches diameter. Native range: tropical Indo-Pacific reef slopes, bays, lagoons. Confirmed Family Discosomatidae, Order Corallimorpharia.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics – Mushroom Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked genus-level Rhodactis parameters. Temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm. Flow low to moderate; feeding once or twice per week. Family Discosomatidae confirmed.

additional.name: Extreme Corals – Rhodactis Mushroom Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters and care: temperature 76–80°F (24–27°C), SG 1.024–1.026, pH 8.0–8.4, alkalinity 7–10 dKH, PAR 75–150. Beginner-to-intermediate care level. Low to moderate indirect flow preferred. Supplemental feeding 1–2x/week noted.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Rhodactis Mushroom Coral Care
additional.notes: Confirmed PAR range 75–150, native range (Indo-Pacific: Fiji, Tonga, Solomon Islands, Great Barrier Reef), size range 1–18 inches across varieties, asexual reproduction via longitudinal fission. Taxonomy: Order Corallimorpharia.
